name: Costa Rica (Republic of Costa Rica / República de Costa Rica)
summary: Costa Rica is a Central American civil-law republic whose family-law framework operates under the Family Code 1973 (Código de Familia, Law 5476) — among the earlier comprehensive Latin American Family Codes. The Family Code was substantively reformed by Law 7689 of 1997, Law 8101 of 2001 (Responsible Parenthood Law) and subsequent modernisation. Parental authority (patria potestad) and child custody are governed by Family Code arts. 140-160. The Supreme Court of Justice (Corte Suprema de Justicia) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters; the Constitutional Chamber (Sala Constitucional) operates within the Supreme Court with constitutional review jurisdiction. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in the Family Courts (Juzgados de Familia). Psychology profession is regulated under the Colegio de Profesionales en Psicología de Costa Rica (Psychology Professional College Law 1977) — among the earliest Latin American statutory psychology professional regulation. Costa Rica is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the superior-interest-of-the-child standard codified in Children and Adolescents Code 1998 art. 5. Costa Rica acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 March 1999.

statutory_framework.title: Family Code — Parental authority and custody
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Family Code among earlier comprehensive Latin American Family Codes. Substantively reformed by Law 7689/1997, Law 8101/2001 (Responsible Parenthood Law). Arts. 140-160 govern patria potestad and child custody.

statutory_framework.title: Children and Adolescents Code
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Children and Adolescents Code aligned with UNCRC obligations codifying superior-interest principle.

statutory_framework.title: Responsible Parenthood Law
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute on responsible parenthood and paternity establishment — structurally distinctive Latin American codification.

statutory_framework.title: Psychology Professional College Law
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute establishing Colegio de Profesionales en Psicología — among earliest Latin American statutory psychology regulation.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of Justice (Corte Suprema de Justicia)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

apex_courts.name: Constitutional Chamber (Sala Constitucional)
apex_courts.role: Constitutional Chamber within Supreme Court with constitutional review jurisdiction.

professional_regulators.name: Colegio de Profesionales en Psicología de Costa Rica
professional_regulators.role: Federal professional college for psychologists in Costa Rica established by Law 6144/1977 — among earliest Latin American statutory psychology regulation.

anonymisation_convention: Costa Rican family-court decisions are anonymised per Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Costa Rican independence + Central-American-Federation framework
key_developments.description: Costa Rican independence 15 September 1821 from Spain + subsequent Central American Federation membership 1823-1838 — substantively distinctive Central American post-Spanish-independence framework. Foundational pre-1948-army-abolition framework affecting subsequent constitutional-administrative-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Costa Rican-Civil-War + army-abolition + 1949-Constitution framework
key_developments.description: Costa Rican Civil War March-April 1948 + José Figueres Ferrer junta + army abolition 1 December 1948 (Article 12 of Constitution) — substantively distinctive globally only-Central-American-state-without-standing-army-since-1948 framework. Constitution of Costa Rica 7 November 1949 establishing presidential republic + Sala Constitucional foundation. Foundational substantive 78-year demilitarised-republic framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Family Code 1973 + early-Latin-American-Family-Code framework
key_developments.description: Federal Family Code 5476 of 21 December 1973 — substantively distinctive globally among-earlier-comprehensive-Latin-American-Family-Codes framework. Foundational substantive Costa-Rican-Family-Code framework persisting through subsequent reform-trajectory affecting family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: Psychology Professional College Law + earliest-Latin-American-statutory-psychology-regulation framework
key_developments.description: Federal Law 6144 of 28 November 1977 establishing Colegio de Profesionales en Psicología de Costa Rica — substantively distinctive globally among-earliest-Latin-American-statutory-psychology-regulation framework. Foundational substantive psychology-profession-regulation framework persisting through subsequent reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Costa Rica ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child framework
key_developments.description: Costa Rica ratified UNCRC 21 August 1990 — substantively significant child-rights framework integration. Foundational superior-interest-of-the-child framework integration with subsequent 1998 Children and Adolescents Code substantive doctrine.

key_developments.title: Family Code Reform Law 7689 + family-law-modernisation framework
key_developments.description: Substantive Family Code reform Law 7689 1997 — substantively significant Central American family-law-modernisation framework. Substantive family-law-implementation framework affecting subsequent 1998 Children and Adolescents Code reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Children and Adolescents Code (Law 7739) + superior-interest-codification framework
key_developments.description: Federal Code (Ley 7739, Código de la Niñez y la Adolescencia) 30 January 1998 enacted codifying superior-interest-of-the-child principle (art. 5) aligned with UNCRC obligations — substantively significant Central American child-protection framework. Foundational substantive children's-rights framework persisting through subsequent reform-trajectory affecting family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession + earlier-Latin-American-Hague-accession framework
key_developments.description: Costa Rica acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 March 1999 — substantively distinctive Central American among-earlier-Latin-American-Hague-accessions framework. Substantive cross-border-jurisdiction-practice framework affecting subsequent reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Responsible Parenthood Law (Law 8101) + earliest-Latin-American-responsible-parenthood-codification framework
key_developments.description: Federal Law 8101 (Ley de Paternidad Responsable) of 27 April 2001 enacted establishing responsible parenthood and paternity establishment as legal obligation — substantively distinctive globally among-earliest-Latin-American-codifications-addressing-responsible-parenthood-and-paternity-establishment-as-legal-obligation framework. Substantive family-law-implementation framework affecting subsequent reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Chaves presidency + PANI institutional framework + same-sex marriage operationalisation
key_developments.description: Rodrigo Chaves Robles (PPSD) presidency inauguration 8 May 2022. Patronato Nacional de la Infancia (PANI) institutional framework addressing PA-related issues within Children and Adolescents Code 1998 + Family Code 1973 framework. Same-sex marriage (effective 26 May 2020) operationalisation continuing through 2022 — Costa Rica distinctive as first Central American state to legalise same-sex marriage. Substantively significant Central American institutional jurisprudential development.

key_developments.title: Corte Suprema de Justicia + Sala Constitucional + Juzgados de Familia — superior-interest-of-the-child substantive register + Chaves presidency framework
key_developments.description: Supreme Court of Justice (Corte Suprema de Justicia), Constitutional Chamber (Sala Constitucional), and Family Courts (Juzgados de Familia) continue to develop superior-interest-of-the-child jurisprudence under Family Code 1973 arts. 140-160 + Children and Adolescents Code 1998 art. 5 + Responsible Parenthood Law 2001 + Hague Convention 1980 framework in custody disputes within Rodrigo Chaves Robles (PPSD) presidency since 8 May 2022. Substantive analysis without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label adoption.

structural_findings: Costa Rica operates a structurally distinctive Spanish-civil-law family-law framework — Family Code 1973 was among earlier comprehensive Latin American Family Codes, and Psychology Professional College Law 1977 is among earliest Latin American statutory psychology regulations in the corpus.
Responsible Parenthood Law 2001 is structurally distinctive — among earliest Latin American codifications addressing responsible parenthood and paternity establishment as legal obligation.
Hague Convention 1980 accession 1999 places Costa Rica as among earlier Latin American Hague accessions in the corpus.

sources.title: Judicial Power of Costa Rica (Poder Judicial)

editorial_notes: Costa Rica jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-09 from 6 to 10 key_developments with full Costa-Rican-independence-1821-to-Chaves-presidency trajectory: 1821-Costa-Rican-independence + 1948-Civil-War-+-army-abolition-+-1949-Constitution + 1973-Family-Code + 1977-Psychology-Professional-College-Law + 1990-Costa-Rica-UNCRC-ratification + 1997-Family-Code-Reform-7689 + 1998-Children-and-Adolescents-Code-7739 + 1999-Hague-Convention-accession + 2001-Responsible-Parenthood-Law-8101 + 2024-Corte-Suprema-+-Sala-Constitucional-+-Chaves-presidency.

Civil-law Central America (Family Code 1973 + Children and Adolescents Code 1998 + Responsible Parenthood Law 2001 + Psychology Professional College Law 1977 — earliest Latin American + Hague Convention 1980 accession 1999). Only Central American state without standing army since 1948 globally distinctive + among earliest Latin American Family Codes + among earliest Latin American statutory psychology regulations + among earliest Latin American Hague accessions.

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive superior-interest-of-the-child analysis under Family Code 1973 arts. 140-160 + Children and Adolescents Code 1998 art. 5 + Responsible Parenthood Law 2001 + Hague Convention 1980 framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.
